pathophysiology_summary: Common warts are benign HPV infections of the skin (typically hands/fingers), presenting as exophytic, rough, dome-shaped hyperkeratotic papules that disrupt skin lines and show pinpoint black dots (thrombosed capillaries) on paring; they spread by autoinoculation (picking/biting) and fomites. They are benign and often self-resolving but slow. The ground-truth task is pattern recognition and differentiation from molluscum contagiosum (smooth, umbilicated, waxy papules), actinic keratosis (flat, sandpapery, sun-damaged skin) and — most importantly — squamous cell carcinoma / amelanotic melanoma in atypical, ulcerated, bleeding, rapidly-growing or irregularly-pigmented lesions (which need biopsy). Management is topical salicylic acid with paring/filing (± cryotherapy) and stopping picking to limit autoinoculation.

differential.evidence_basis: Rough verrucous papules with black dots spreading with picking.
Consider for atypical, ulcerated, bleeding, rapidly-growing or pigmented lesions — biopsy.
Smooth umbilicated waxy papules — argues against (these are rough/verrucous).
Flat sandpapery lesions on sun-damaged skin in older patients — not this morphology/age.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Common warts lead; malignancy must be considered for atypical lesions before reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic verrucous papules with black dots, no malignant/infective features — benign common warts.
No atypical features; biopsy any non-resolving/atypical lesion.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative malignancy/infection screen, benign common warts lead; salicylic acid + stop picking, with a malignancy safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; benign; weeks-months to clear; biopsy if atypical/non-resolving.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign common warts.

red_flags.description: Malignant features / secondary infection — ulceration, spontaneous bleeding, rapid growth, irregular pigmentation or friability (SCC/melanoma), or a hot red swollen area with a red streak/fever (infection)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Atypical lesions need biopsy to exclude malignancy; secondary infection needs treatment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not considering SCC/melanoma in an atypical/non-resolving/pigmented/ulcerated lesion (biopsy).
Continued picking/biting spreads warts by autoinoculation.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Topical salicylic acid + filing + stop picking; cryotherapy via GP; biopsy any atypical/non-resolving lesion; GP for secondary infection.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing a skin cancer masquerading as a wart.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Common warts are benign and often self-resolving; topical salicylic acid with paring/filing is first-line, cryotherapy an option. Avoid picking (autoinoculation). Biopsy atypical or non-resolving lesions to exclude malignancy.
